title: Principles of Inheritance and Variation
subtitle: From Mendelian ratios to pedigree analysis, linkage, mutation and genetic disorders - a visual NEET-ready genetics studio.

heroModel.title: Inheritance logic lab
heroModel.subtitle: Drag to rotate - scroll to zoom

roadmapTitle: Genetics moves from crosses to chromosomes to human disorders.
roadmapDescription: Keep the flow simple: Mendel observed ratios, chromosomes explained them, linkage modified them, pedigree tracks them, and mutation changes them.
flowchart:
flowchart LR
  A[Mendel experiments] --> B[Law of dominance]
  B --> C[Segregation]
  C --> D[Independent assortment]
  D --> E[Extensions of Mendelism]
  E --> F[Chromosomal theory]
  F --> G[Linkage and recombination]
  G --> H[Pedigree analysis]
  H --> I[Genetic disorders]

roadmap.title: Mendelian inheritance
roadmap.summary: Monohybrid and dihybrid crosses reveal predictable inheritance ratios.

roadmap.title: Laws of inheritance
roadmap.summary: Dominance, segregation and independent assortment explain allele behavior.

roadmap.title: Beyond Mendel
roadmap.summary: Incomplete dominance, codominance, multiple alleles and polygenic inheritance modify simple ratios.

roadmap.title: Chromosomal theory
roadmap.summary: Genes are located on chromosomes and move during meiosis.

roadmap.title: Linkage and recombination
roadmap.summary: Linked genes travel together, while crossing over creates new combinations.

roadmap.title: Sex determination
roadmap.summary: XY, XO, ZW and haplodiploid systems decide sex in different organisms.

roadmap.title: Pedigree analysis
roadmap.summary: Family trees help trace autosomal and sex-linked traits.

roadmap.title: Genetic disorders
roadmap.summary: Mendelian and chromosomal disorders arise from gene or chromosome changes.

conceptDescription: Use each card as a small mental model: allele pair, gamete split, fertilisation, phenotype ratio, exception or disorder.

sections.title: Mendelian crosses and test cross
sections.description: Mendel used pea plant traits with clear alternatives and followed inheritance across generations.
points: Monohybrid cross tracks one character and gives 3:1 phenotype ratio in F2 for complete dominance.
Genotypic ratio in monohybrid F2 is 1:2:1.
Dihybrid F2 ratio is 9:3:3:1 when genes assort independently.
Test cross uses a recessive parent to reveal unknown genotype.
sections.neet: Test cross ratio 1:1 suggests heterozygous monohybrid parent.

sections.title: Dominance, segregation and independent assortment
sections.description: Mendelian laws describe how alleles express and separate during gamete formation.
points: Dominance: one allele can mask another in heterozygous condition.
Segregation: allele pairs separate during gamete formation.
Independent assortment: alleles of different genes assort independently if unlinked.
Segregation has no exception among Mendelian laws.
sections.neet: Law of segregation is also called purity of gametes.

sections.title: Incomplete dominance and codominance
sections.description: Some traits do not follow simple dominant-recessive expression.
points: Incomplete dominance gives an intermediate phenotype in heterozygote.
Codominance expresses both alleles together.
AB blood group shows codominance between IA and IB.
Multiple alleles mean more than two allelic forms exist in a population.
sections.neet: ABO blood group involves multiple alleles and codominance.

sections.title: Chromosomal theory of inheritance
sections.description: Sutton and Boveri connected Mendel factors with chromosome behavior during meiosis.
points: Chromosomes occur in pairs like alleles.
Homologous chromosomes segregate during meiosis.
Gametes receive one chromosome from each homologous pair.
Fertilisation restores diploid chromosome number.
sections.neet: Chromosomal movement during meiosis physically explains Mendelian segregation.

sections.title: Linkage, recombination and mapping
sections.description: Genes on the same chromosome may not assort independently.
points: Linked genes are inherited together more often.
Crossing over produces recombinants.
Recombination frequency helps map gene distance.
More distance between genes usually means more recombination.
sections.neet: One map unit equals 1 percent recombination frequency.

sections.title: Sex determination systems
sections.description: Different organisms use different chromosome systems for sex determination.
points: Humans follow XX-XY system.
Grasshopper shows XX-XO system.
Birds follow ZZ-ZW system.
Honeybee uses haplodiploidy where males are haploid.
sections.neet: Father decides the sex of a human child because sperm can carry X or Y.

sections.title: Pedigree analysis
sections.description: Pedigree charts track inheritance of traits through families.
points: Autosomal dominant traits can appear in every generation.
Autosomal recessive traits may skip generations.
X-linked recessive traits are more frequent in males.
Carrier females can transmit X-linked traits.
sections.neet: A criss-cross pattern is common in X-linked inheritance.

sections.title: Mendelian and chromosomal disorders
sections.description: Genetic disorders may result from single gene changes or chromosome number changes.
points: Sickle-cell anaemia is autosomal recessive.
Haemophilia is X-linked recessive.
Down syndrome is trisomy 21.
Turner syndrome is XO and Klinefelter syndrome is XXY.
sections.neet: Down syndrome is caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21.

modelTitle: Rotate genetics as structure, not just ratios.
modelDescription: Original 3D schematics connect allele position, crossing over, pedigree flow and inheritance logic.

models.title: Allele and Punnett lab
models.description: Alleles on chromosomes plus Punnett square logic.

models.title: Gene expression anchor
models.description: How inherited DNA becomes trait-making product.

models.title: Population genetics curve
models.description: Allele frequency idea as population change.

highYield: Monohybrid phenotypic ratio is 3:1 and genotypic ratio is 1:2:1.
Dihybrid ratio is 9:3:3:1 for independent assortment.
Law of segregation has no exception.
ABO blood group shows multiple alleles and codominance.

mistakes.title: Phenotype vs genotype ratio
mistakes.body: Monohybrid F2 phenotype is 3:1, but genotype is 1:2:1.

mistakes.title: Dominance is not strength
mistakes.body: Dominant allele is expressed in heterozygote; it is not necessarily more common or biologically stronger.

mistakes.title: Linkage modifies assortment
mistakes.body: Linked genes do not show independent assortment unless crossing over separates them.

quiz.question: The genotypic ratio of a monohybrid F2 cross is:
quiz.explain: Complete dominance gives 1 homozygous dominant : 2 heterozygous : 1 homozygous recessive.

quiz.question: A test cross is performed with:
options: An unknown dominant parent and another dominant parent
quiz.explain: The recessive parent reveals whether the dominant-looking individual is homozygous or heterozygous.

quiz.question: AB blood group is an example of:
quiz.explain: IA and IB are expressed together.

quiz.question: Linked genes are found on:
quiz.explain: Genes on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together.

quiz.question: Down syndrome is caused by:
quiz.explain: Down syndrome is trisomy of chromosome 21.

quiz.question: Haemophilia is usually:
quiz.explain: NCERT treats haemophilia as X-linked recessive.

quiz.question: One map unit equals:
quiz.explain: A centimorgan/map unit corresponds to 1 percent recombination.

quiz.question: Law of segregation is also called:
quiz.explain: Alleles separate so gametes carry one allele.

quiz.question: Sickle-cell anaemia is:
quiz.explain: Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosomal recessive Mendelian disorder.

flashcards.back: Cross with homozygous recessive parent to identify genotype.
Both alleles express in heterozygote, e.g. AB blood group.
Genes on same chromosome inherited together more often.
New allele combinations due to crossing over.
Gain or loss of chromosomes, e.g. trisomy 21.
Family chart used to trace trait inheritance.
